coredata.dc:title: Integration of orange peel derived carbon quantum dots with TiO2 for enhanced photocatalytic degradation of brilliant green dye

coredata.dc:description: Brilliant green (BG) dye is a non-biodegradable, highly venomous and coloured pigment. BG dye possesses remarkably high visibility even at small concentration that causes diarrhea and abdominal pain in humans when being ingested. In addition, it is harmful to living organisms and polluting aquatic environments. Hence, eliminating BG dye from contaminated water is an urgent need. In this report, a photocatalyst encompasses hydrothermally prepared titanium dioxide (TiO2) integrated with orange peel derived oxygenated carbon quantum dots (O-CQDs) was developed for effective removal of BG dye. The O-CQDs were prepared using a single step eco-friendly hydrothermal process and an intense green fluorescence was observed upon exposing the O-CQDs to far UV light irradiation (254 nm). The formation of TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid was confirmed through UV–Visible spectrophotometer, X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses. A random distribution of spherical shaped O-CQDs on the surface of TiO2 was clearly observed in FESEM images. The carboxyl groups present in O-CQDs not only increased interlayer spacing but also led to the attachment of TiO2 effectively. The TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid showed 94.70 % efficiency towards photocatalytic degradation of BG dye under UV light irradiation that was ∼20 % higher than the efficiency of pristine TiO2 photocatalyst. The presence of O-CQDs in the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid boosted the photocatalytic degradation efficiency by enhancing UV light absorption and suppressing photo-generated electron–hole recombination. The integration of agricultural waste derived carbon with TiO2 by a facile synthesis method paves a way for large scale synthesis of TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid, which is anticipated to broaden its application potential in environmental remediation through photocatalytic degradation of organic compounds.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of hydrothermally formed titanium dioxide integrated with orange peel derived oxygenated carbon quantum dots and their application in the photocatalytic degradation of brilliant green dye are demonstrated.

Brilliant green (BG) dye is a non-biodegradable, highly venomous and coloured pigment. BG dye possesses remarkably high visibility even at small concentration that causes diarrhea and abdominal pain in humans when being ingested. In addition, it is harmful to living organisms and polluting aquatic environments. Hence, eliminating BG dye from contaminated water is an urgent need. In this report, a photocatalyst encompasses hydrothermally prepared titanium dioxide (TiO2) integrated with orange peel derived oxygenated carbon quantum dots (O-CQDs) was developed for effective removal of BG dye. The O-CQDs were prepared using a single step eco-friendly hydrothermal process and an intense green fluorescence was observed upon exposing the O-CQDs to far UV light irradiation (254 nm). The formation of TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid was confirmed through UV–Visible spectrophotometer, X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses. A random distribution of spherical shaped O-CQDs on the surface of TiO2 was clearly observed in FESEM images. The carboxyl groups present in O-CQDs not only increased interlayer spacing but also led to the attachment of TiO2 effectively. The TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid showed 94.70 % efficiency towards photocatalytic degradation of BG dye under UV light irradiation that was ∼20 % higher than the efficiency of pristine TiO2 photocatalyst. The presence of O-CQDs in the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid boosted the photocatalytic degradation efficiency by enhancing UV light absorption and suppressing photo-generated electron–hole recombination. The integration of agricultural waste derived carbon with TiO2 by a facile synthesis method paves a way for large scale synthesis of TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid, which is anticipated to broaden its application potential in environmental remediation through photocatalytic degradation of organic compounds.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been emerged as versatile and powerful tool in various technological applications [1], such as photovoltaics [2], bioimaging [3–5] and environmental remediation [6–8]. Different types of materials have recently been employed for a variety of photocatalytic processes [9–11]. Precisely, CQDs play a crucial role as co-catalyst or sensitizer in conjunction with semiconductor photocatalysts, like titanium dioxide (TiO2) [12]. In addition, graphitic carbon nitride/metal oxide-based heterojunctions have also been contributed for significant advancements in photocatalytic applications [13,14]. When exposed to light, semiconductor photocatalysts generate electron–hole (e−–h+) pairs, which can initiate redox reactions, leading to degradation of organic pollutants, viz. dyes in water or air environment. The CQDs are compatible with biopolymers and various metals [15] and consequently form a non-toxic fluorescent material suitable for application as photocatalyst. A variety of CQDs have been synthesized and utilized for detecting and removing synthetic dyes [15,16] and hazardous waste pollutants from wastewater [17,18]. Synthetic dyes liberated from textile, paper and plastic industries are among the most alarming pollutants that need to be removed without compromise as they pose severe health risks to all levels of organisms [19]. Although several technologies exist, finding more efficient methods to remove synthetic dyes is still a burgeoning area of research. The combination of CQDs and TiO2 as a composite displays enhanced efficacy towards photocatalytic degradation of various dyes [20,21]. The CQDs sourced from agricultural waste were utilized alongside TiO2 for photocatalytic degradation purposes [22]. The cost of fabrication of the CQDs/metal oxide nanocomposites can be overcome by utilising CQDs derived from agricultural waste through green synthesis process.

The CQDs synthesized from agricultural waste using green methods also showed greater potential in environmental remediation, bioimaging and biosensing applications [23]. The CQDs extracted from agricultural waste are zero-dimensional, fluorescent, biocompatible and exhibit high photostability, along with tunable photoluminescence [24]. For instance, the CQDs derived from Citrus grandis fruit juice were employed for the removal of methylene blue dye [25] and the CQDs prepared from Ocimum basilium L. seed extract were applied for the degradation of methyl orange, methylene blue, malachite green and crystal violet dyes [26]. Though CQDs synthesized from agricultural waste demonstrated considerable efficiency in the photocatalytic degradation of dyes, their effectiveness was enhanced when employed as CQDs/metal oxide nanocomposites. Consequently, the CQDs/nickel oxide nanocomposite and ZnO/CQDs/AgNPs were successfully synthesized and applied for the removal of malachite green [27] and methylene blue [28] dyes, respectively.

While recent efforts have aimed at developing greener synthesis methods [29], there remains a gap in establishing a simple and single step method [30] that utilizes abundant and renewable waste materials for producing photocatalysts. In particular, the potential of orange peel powder [31,32] has been employed for this purpose due to its availability and rich organic content. This study presents a novel, sustainable, efficient and one-step hydrothermal synthesis method for the preparation of oxygenated carbon quantum dots (O-CQDs) from orange peel powder, which is a frequently discarded agricultural waste material. It also explores the improvement of TiO2 photocatalytic capability by fabricating a TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid through an eco-friendly process and assessing its efficacy towards photocatalytic degradation of brilliant green (BG) dye. An enhanced light absorption and suppression of photogenerated electron–hole recombination owned by the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid apparently reflected in improvement in its efficiency towards photocatalytic degradation of BG dye.

Orange peels were collected from the local market, Krishnankoil, Virudhunagar District, Tamil Nadu, India. Sulphuric acid (H2SO4), sodium hypochlorite (NaOCl), ethanol (C2H5OH), titanium (IV) isopropoxide (Ti[OCH(CH3)2]4), hydrofluoric acid (HF) and brilliant green (BG) dye were procured from Sigma Aldrich. Dichloromethane (CH2Cl2), ammonium oxalate (AO), benzoquinone (BQ) and isopropanol (IPA) were purchased from Merck. All the chemicals were used as received. Distilled water was used for all the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of oxygenated carbon quantum dots (O-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The orange peels collected from the local market were washed using water, dried under sunlight and then oven dried at 100 °C overnight. The dried orange peels were crushed to powder and then the powder was sieved to attain even size. Accurately weighed (2 g) of the dried orange peel powder was treated with 0.1 M H2SO4 for 30 min and was washed by plenty of water for 4–7 times. It was further treated with NaOCl solution for 30 min and again washed using water and ethanol to attain a pH value of 7. Consequently, the orange peel powder was changed to white jelly type mass, which was transferred to Teflon lined autoclave containing 80 mL of distilled water and 20 mL of ethanol. The autoclave was heat treated at 180 °C for 12 h and was allowed to cool down at room temperature naturally. The resultant sample was collected and washed using CH2Cl2 to remove unreacted organic moieties. The resultant aqueous O-CQDs solution was dark in color and showed green fluorescence under UV light irradiation (Fig. S1). The sample solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 25 min to separate unreacted residues. The collected supernatant liquid containing O-CQDs was kept in a rotary evaporator for 15 min to remove ethanol. About 95 % of the ethanol used in this hydrothermal process was recovered back and utilized again. The ethanol free O-CQDs were recovered by freeze drying method.

A mixture containing 25 mL of Ti[OCH(CH3)2]4 with 6 mL of 40 wt% HF solution was subjected to hydrothermal reaction at 180 °C for 24 h in Teflon lined autoclave. Then, the reaction mixture was allowed to cool at room temperature. A colorless precipitate was formed at bottom of the autoclave, which was separated from supernatant liquid by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 20 min. The separated precipitate was washed repeatedly using distilled water and ethanol to remove any excess fluoride content. The fluoride-free TiO2 precipitate was dried at 80 °C for 6 h and then subjected to calcination at 600 °C.

About 10 mL of freeze-dried O-CQDs with 50 mL of ethanol was added to 100 mg of TiO2 and subjected to sonication for 30 min. The sonicated reaction mixture containing TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid was dried at 80 °C for 6 h. Scheme 1 illustrates steps involved in the synthesis of TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid.

A 40 mg of TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid was added to 100 mL of BG dye (20 ppm) and subjected to vigorous stirring for 1 h in dark environment to get adsorption/desorption balance between BG dye and TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid photocatalyst. A Heber multi photoreactor with a cut off filter (λ > 365 nm) was employed as a light source. The photocatalytic reaction mixture (TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid + BG dye) was exposed to UV light irradiation for 45 min under stirring. A small quantity of aliquots (5 mL) from the photocatalytic reaction mixture was collected after every 5 min of the UV light irradiation. The TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid photocatalyst was recovered from the photocatalytic reaction medium by centrifugation at 1000 rpm for 10 min. The recovered photocatalyst was washed repeatedly using water and ethanol till the adsorbed BG dye was desorbed into the aqueous solution and dried the recovered photocatalyst at 60 °C overnight for subsequent recycling experiments.

Holes (h+), superoxide radicals (O2 •−) and hydroxyl radicals (OH•) are the major contributing agents for the photocatalytic reaction. To evaluate the influence of these three active agents on the photocatalytic degradation of BG dye by TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid, quenching experiments with AO for h+, BQ for O2 •− and IPA for OH• were performed.

Absorption characteristics of O-CQDs and TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid were studied using Jasco V650 UV–Visible spectrophotometer. Information on crystallinity was obtained by X-ray diffractometer (Bruker D8 Advance ECO). Functional groups present in the O-CQDs, TiO2 and TiO2/O-CQDs were identified by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Shimadzu) between the wavelength range of 4000 and 400 cm−1 with a resolution of 16 cm−1. Raman analysis of the O-CQDs was performed using confocal Raman spectrometer (WITEC). Morphology and elemental composition of the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid was examined using Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) coupled with energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) accessory (Carl Zeiss Sigma 300). Surface area, pore diameter and pore volume of the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid were measured from Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms recorded using Autosorb IQ Series surface area analyser (Quantachrome Instruments). State of chemical compositions in the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid was determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; PHI – VERSAPROBE III).

Fig. 1 shows UV–Visible absorption spectra of the O-CQDs and TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid. The absorption spectrum of O-CQDs displays a broad absorption band from 215 nm–295 nm, corresponding to the π-π* transition that is attributed to the existance of CC bond in the O-CQDs [33]. A shoulder peak was observed at 312 nm indicating the presence of CO groups on the surface of O-CQDs [34]. The absorption spectrum of TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid also shows a braod absorption band in the wavelength range of 205 nm–305 nm similar to that of O-CQDs, infering the existance of CC and CO groups in the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid.

XRD pattern of O-CQDs shows a broad diffraction peak revealing that the nature of CQDs formed from orange peel extract was in amorphous phase (Fig. 2 (a)). The predominant diffraction peak observed at 24.90° with d-spacing of 0.37 nm reveals increase in the d-spacing compared to graphitic carbon (0.33 nm), due to the existence of carbonyl and epoxy linkages between the lattices [35,36]. The minor diffraction peaks noticed at 43.09° and 63.00° also infer the presence of oxygenated functional groups on the surface of O-CQDs [37]. The XRD pattern of TiO2 confirms the presence of pure anatase phase TiO2 without any impurity peaks (Fig. 2(b)). The diffraction peaks observed at 25.56°, 38.06°, 48.32°, 54.18°, 55.28°, 62.65°, 68.78°, 70.24° and 75.10° correspond to the (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204), (116), (220) and (215) crystal planes, respectively of the anatase phase TiO2 (JCPDS Card No. 21-1272). The diffraction peaks detected for the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid disclose that the inclusion of O-CQDs did not significantly alter crystal lattice of the anatase phase TiO2 (Fig. 2(c)). The lattice parameters previously reported for the pristine TiO2 were a = b = 3.7748 Å and c = 9.4083 Å [38]. Nevertheless, in the present study, the inclusion of O-CQDs in the TiO2 matrix reveals a very minimal variation in the lattice parameters (a = b = 3.7904 Å and c = 9.5325 Å). This infers that the addition of O-CQDs to the anatase phase TiO2 to form TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid did not make any remarkable changes in the lattice parameters of TiO2. However, the intensity of diffraction peaks in the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid increased significantly with slight broadening in the width, owing to the presence of amorphous O-CQDs [39]. The absence of diffraction peaks relevant to O-CQDs in the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid may be due to its minimal concentration and high degree of dispersion with poor crystallinity [40].

FTIR spectral analysis provides information about functional groups present in the O-CQDs, TiO2 and TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid. The FTIR spectrum of O-CQDs displays peak at 3434 cm−1 corresponds to OH stretching, 2919 and 2850 cm−1 for CH stretching vibration, 1635 cm−1 corresponds to CO stretching and 1096 cm−1 corresponds to C-O stretching, which confirm the presence of oxygenated groups on the surface of O-CQDs (Fig. 3 (a)). The peak observed at around 715 cm−1 corresponds to CH bending vibration of sp2 hybridized carbon structure [41]. In the FTIR spectrum of TiO2 (Fig. 3(b)), the appearance of peak at 3437 cm−1 corresponds to OH stretching, 1600 cm−1 corresponds to stretching of water molecule associated with TiO2 and the peak noticed at 1405 cm−1 corresponds to stretching vibration of TiO [42]. A broad absorption band observed between 450 and 800 cm−1 can be ascribed to vibration of TiOTi linkage in the anatase phase TiO2 [43]. In the FTIR spectrum of TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid (Fig. 3(c)), typical peaks corresponding to CH stretching present in the O-CQDs and anatase phase TiO2 were not seen instead, a new peak was observed at 2350 cm−1. This can be attributed to CO stretching vibrations contributed by the O-CQDs, suggesting the existence of oxygenated groups in the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid. The peaks noticed at 1596, 1405 and 1103 cm−1 also reveal the presence of CO groups in the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid. The peak corresponding to TiOTi bending vibrations present in the XRD between 400 and 800 cm−1 was similar to that of anatase phase TiO2. This peak confirms the structural integrity of the TiO2 framework within the nanohybrid. The presence of TiOTi bending vibrations in the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid restricts change in lattice parameters of the anatase phase TiO2 and therefore, the crystal lattice remains largely unaffected by the introduction of O-CQDs. These results are in correlation with the corresponding XRD results. The broad peaks positioned between 3300 and 3600 cm−1 corresponding to OH stretching were found commonly in the O-CQDs, TiO2 and TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid that reveals the existence of hydroxyl group.

Raman spectrum of orange peel derived O-CQDs is presented in Fig. 4 . The peak observed at 1065 cm−1 corresponds to CH bending vibration or CO streching mode, which implies that oxygenated functional groups are present on the surface of O-CQDs. This phenomenon is common in CQDs synthesized from agricultural waste materials, due to the presence of these functional groups in the precursor material (orange peel). The peak detected at 1298 cm−1 can be attributed to D-band for secondary disorder related to vibrations of carbon [44]. This can also be related to structural disorder occurred due to the incorporation of hetero atoms into the lattice of carbon. This futhur confirms the fuctionalization of CQDs synthesised from orange peel extract by single step hydrothermal process. The peak noticed at 1558 cm−1 can be attributed to G band and it is sp2 hybridized carbon atom in amorphous carbon phase.

The morphology of TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid was analyzed using FESEM images (Fig. 5 (a–d)). The FESEM images of the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid confirm the formation of tetrahedral anatase phase TiO2 and spherical shaped O-CQDs. In the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid, the O-CQDs are randomly distributed on the surface of TiO2. The O-CQDs are visible as tiny white spots as their size and concentration are very low [45]. The peaks obtained from the EDX analysis confirms the presence of elements, such as C, O and Ti in the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid without any impurities (Fig. S2). The weight percentages of C, O and Ti elements in the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid are found to be 14.79, 48.67 and 36.54 %, respectively and their atomic percentages are determined to be 24.45, 60.40 and 15.15 %, respectively.

Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) analysis has been established as an ideal method to determine specific surface area, pore diameter and pore volume of nanomaterials [46]. Consequently, nitrogen adsorption–desorption analysis of the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid was conducted at 77 K by adopting BET adsorption model. Generally, the photocatalytic ability is directly related to specific surface area and pore size of the catalyst. The nitrogen (N2) adsorption–desorption isotherms and the associated pore size distribution curve of the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid are illustrated in Fig. 6 . The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid displayed type IV isotherm with H3 hysteresis loop demonstrating mesoporous nature with aggregation of particles [47]. From Fig. 6(a), the BET specific surface area of the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid was found to be 66.2 m2 g−1. The pore size distribution curve (Fig. 6(b)) unveils that the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid has a narrow pore diameter of 20 nm. The Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) pore volume of the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid was found to be 0.13 cm3 g−1. The mesoporosity with high surface area of the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid would be beneficial for providing maximum accessibility of active sites by the BG dye molecules for efficient photocatalytic reaction.

The state of chemical compositions in the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid was determined by XPS analysis (Fig. 7 ). The XPS survey spectrum of the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid (Fig. 7(a)) showed three major peaks indicating the existence of carbon (C) (35.03 %), titanium (Ti) (19.81 %) and oxygen (O) (44.58 %) in the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid. The high-resolution C 1s spectra (Fig. 7(b)) showed three deconvoluted binding energy peaks at 284.7, 285.9 and 288.6 eV, corresponding to CC/CC, CO and CO bonds, respectively. The deconvoluted Ti 2p spectra (Fig. 7(c)) exhibited two binding energy peaks at 459.0 and 464.5 eV associated with Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, respectively that are harmony with previously reported values [39]. The deconvoluted O 1s spectra (Fig. 7(d)) displayed three peaks at 530.5, 531.6 and 532.5 eV, which can be assigned to TiO, CO and CO bonds, respectively. These results evidently proved that O-CQDs are eventually mixed with TiO2 via a chemical (TiOC) bonding between TiO2 and O-CQDs [48,49].

The adsorption property followed by the photocatalytic ability of the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid photocatalyst was investigated towards degradation of BG dye. Initially, the BG dye was subjected to self-degradation without TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid photocatalyst under UV light irradiation for 4 h and observed a degradation efficiency of 9.12 % (Fig. S3). In contrast, the degradation efficiency was increased to 74.46 % in the presence of anatase TiO2 photocatalyst (Fig. S4). The minimal self-degradation (9.12 %) and 74.46 % degradation of BG dye by the anatase TiO2 photocatalyst emphasized the need for a photocatalyst with enhanced degradation efficiency. In this context, the degradation of the BG dye (20 ppm) was performed in the presence of TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid photocatalyst. The TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid and BG dye was stirred vigorously for 1 h prior to UV light irradiation to reach a stable adsorption/desorption equilibrium [50]. It was found that the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid significantly enhanced the photocatalytic degradation of BG dye and maximum photocatalytic degradation efficiency of 94.70 % was obtained for a photocatalytic dosage of 40 mg by varying the degradation time from 0 min to 45 min (Fig. 8 (a)). The change in degradation efficiency of the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid photocatalyst with respect to time is given in Table 1 . Diverse quantities of TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid photocatalyst (10, 20, 30, 40 and 50 mg) were used for the degradation of BG dye (Fig. 8(b)) and the obtained photocatalytic degradation efficiency are presented in Table S1. Increasing the amount of photocatalyst above 40 mg might enhanced the adsorption of BG dye but affected the penetration of UV light inside the photocatalytic reaction medium that ultimately reduced the degradation efficiency. Thus, the amount of TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid photocatalyst was optimized (40 mg) by which a maximum degradation of BG dye was attained in 45 min. The TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid exhibited significantly enhanced photocatalytic activity when compared to anatase TiO2 (Fig. 9 (a)). This improved photocatalytic performance of the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid can be attributed to several factors. The presence of O-CQDs enhances light absorption and charge separation efficiency, which are critical for photocatalytic process. The O-CQDs also provides additional active sites for the adsorption of BG dye molecules that facilitates effective degradation. Moreover, the interaction between O-CQDs and TiO2 reduces rate of electron–hole recombination that further boosts the photocatalytic activity [51]. All these observations suggested that the proposed TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid photocatalyst not only accelerates degradation process of the BG dye under UV light irradiation but also offers a more robust and efficient solution when compared to pristine TiO2. The comparison of photocatalytic performance of the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid with earlier reports towards degradation of BG dye is given in Table 2 .

The kinetic curve for the photocatalytic degradation of BG dye by the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid under UV light irradiation is illustrated in Fig. 9(b). The plots of −ln(C/C0) against irradiation time (t) gives a linear pattern with good correlation coefficient (R2 ≥ 0.98), indicating that the reaction kinetics follow a pseudo-first-order model [56]. The degradation rate constant was determined by calculating the slope of the fitted straight line. In the presence of TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid photocatalyst, the BG degradation rate constant was found to be 0.0612 min−1. The proportion of BG dye removal by the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid catalyst under dark condition was evaluated for 45 min to prove the effect of light and the result is given in Fig. S5. It is observed from Fig. S5 that the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid catalyst could only remove 4.06 % of BG dye under dark in 45 min, chiefly due to the adsorption of BG dye molecules on the surface of TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid catalyst. This confirms that the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid is highly active only under UV light and as a consequence it degrades the BG dye efficiently by means of photochemical reaction mechanism. Meanwhile, to ascertain the effect of BG dye on the degradation process under UV light irradiation, the concentration of BG dye was varied from 10 ppm to 40 ppm by maintaining same amount of catalyst (40 mg) (Fig. S6). When the photocatalytic experiment was conducted with BG dye concentration of 10 ppm, the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid catalyst exhibited degradation efficiency of 92.85 %. When the BG dye concentration was increased form 10 ppm to 20 ppm the photocatalytic degradation efficiency was also increased from 92.85 % to 94.70 %. Whereas, further increasing the BG dye concentration from 20 ppm to 30 ppm and 40 ppm resulted in decrease in the photocatalytic degradation efficiency from 94.70 % to 86.13 % and 76.21 %, respectively. The decrement in the photocatalytic degradation efficiency can be attributed to blocking of active sites of the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid catalyst by the excess BG dye molecules [57]. Hence, 20 ppm BG dye concentration was chosen as the optimum concentration for this study. The comparison of photocatalytic performance of TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid with O-CQDs modified TiO2 photocatalysts reported previously towards the degradation of different organic pollutants is given in Table S2.

The plausible photocatalytic degradation mechanism of BG dye by TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid is illustrated in Scheme 2 . In aqueous medium, the BG dye exists in its cationic form, while the surface of the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid is negatively charged [58] that enable effective adsorption followed by degradation of BG dye. The TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid catalytic system exhibits an increased light absorption capacity due to the presence of O-CQDs that persuades creation of holes (h+) and electrons (e−) effectively in TiO2 with the support of CQDs. Additionally, the O-CQDs can easily accept the e− from the conduction band of TiO2 NPs that helps to avoid reunion of e− and h+ in the TiO2 NPs [59]. Thus, creation of h+ and e− with high separation ability in TiO2 NPs and blocking of rejoining process by O-CQDs enhance the interaction of e− with O2 to produce O2 •− species and the interaction of h+ with reaction medium of water produces OH• species. The so formed highly reactive species react with BG dye to deliver mineralized products during the complete degradation process [60]. The O-CQDs not only enhance the light absorption of TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid but also improve the charge separation and prolong the lifetime of the photoexcited charge carriers. The efficient generation and utilization of reactive species are key factors for the enhanced photocatalytic performance of the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid, making it an effective solution for degrading organic pollutants in wastewater. The detailed photocatalytic degradation process of BG dye by TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid is represented in the following equations.

The reusability of the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid photocatalyst for the degradation of BG dye was evaluated through recycling experiments under UV light irradiation by repetition of photocatalytic experiments five times using the recovered photocatalyst (Fig. S7). Impressively, the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid sustain its photocatalytic activity by exhibiting efficiencies of 94.70, 91.52, 87.14, 83.61 and 80.35 % during the first, second, third, fourth and fifth recycling experiments, respectively, indicating minimal deactivation of the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid [61]. This observed degradation efficiency after five cycles suggests that the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid photocatalyst maintains its integrity and performance over multiple uses. The steady decrement in the photocatalytic efficiency of the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid photocatalyst can be ascribed to leaching of active sites on the surface of catalyst and catalyst loss during the recovery of photocatalyst [62,63].

Three major active substances involved in the photocatalytic degradation of dye molecules are photogenerated holes (h+), superoxide radicals (O2 •−) and hydroxyl radicals (OH•). To investigate the contribution of these species in the photocatalytic reaction, specific scavengers were employed: AO for h+, IPA for OH• and BQ for O2 •− [64]. When compared to the reaction without scavengers, the photocatalytic activity of the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid was inhibited considerably when these scavengers were added. The photocatalytic degradation efficiency of TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid was decreased significantly from 94.70 % to 37.86 % with the addition of AO, demonstrating that photogenerated holes are primary active species for the photocatalytic reaction. Similarly, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid showed a substantial decrease (50.83 %) after the addition of IPA, illustrating that hydroxyl radicals (OH•) also play a significant role towards the photocatalytic degradation of BG dye. In contrast, the degradation efficiency in the presence of BQ was 70.39 %, suggesting that the superoxide radicals (O2 •−) have relatively major effect on the photocatalytic degradation process (Fig. 10 ). The relative contributions of the oxidative species towards the degradation of BG dye molecules are as follows: holes (h+) > hydroxyl radicals (OH•) > superoxide radicals (O2 •−). This hierarchy underscores the importance of photogenerated holes and hydroxyl radicals for the photocatalytic process [65]. Hence, understanding the specific roles and effectiveness of these active species is crucial for optimizing the performance of the proposed TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid photocatalyst in degrading organic pollutants.

Sustainable development of TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid photocatalyst was made through orange peel extract derived O-CQDs and anatase TiO2 by hydrothermal and sonication processes. The UV–Visible, XRD, FTIR, Raman, FESEM, EDX and XPS analyses evidenced the successful integration of O-CQDs with TiO2. The negative charge on the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid provided active sites for the effective adsorption of BG dye. In addition, improved light absorption and charge separation efficiency provided by the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid enhanced the overall photocatalytic degradation of the BG dye. Further, the O-CQDs served as an electron sink to arrest the electron–hole recombination process thereby increased the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of the TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid. A satisfactory result was obtained through reusable tests and the role of active species towards the BG dye degradation process was also analyzed by radical scavenging study. This enhanced performance of the proposed TiO2/O-CQDs nanohybrid photocatalyst makes it more promising candidate for environmental remediation applications.
